code_snippet: def precompute_kpis(cd_cvm: int, years_tuple: tuple) -> dict:
description: precompute_kpis() is ~210 lines with two nested loops (year iteration + KPI calculation), inline helper closures (_g, _gsum), and 50+ KPI assignments in a single dict literal. It has at least 3 distinct responsibilities: raw value extraction, KPI derivation, and horizontal analysis. This makes it very hard to unit test individual KPI groups.
recommendation: Extract at minimum: (1) a `_extract_raw_values(ay, df, y)` function returning the raw dict, (2) a `_compute_derived_kpis(r, prev_r)` function for KPI calculation, (3) `_horizontal_analysis(r, r_prev, sorted_years, i)` for YoY/CAGR. precompute_kpis() becomes a thin orchestrator.

description: The comment '3 anos' is factually wrong for the formula used (see CORR-003). Misleading comments are worse than no comments — they cause future maintainers to trust incorrect documentation.
recommendation: Update comment to match the formula: '# CAGR receita (2 anos: sorted_years[i-2] → sorted_years[i])'.

description: The helper closures _g() and _gsum() are re-defined identically inside both precompute_kpis() and precompute_quarterly_kpis(). This is ~12 lines of duplicated code that must be kept in sync. The same pattern appears a third time in compute_peer_kpis() and load_heatmap_data().
recommendation: Extract into a module-level function `get_kpi_value(df, names, mode='first'|'sum')` that takes a DataFrame slice and returns the value.

code_snippet:
yy = str(year)[2:]; errors = []
c1, c2, c3, c4, ca = f'1Q{yy}', f'2Q{yy}', f'3Q{yy}', f'4Q{yy}', str(year)
h1, h2, h3, ha = c1 in df.columns, c2 in df.columns, ...
description: Single-letter variable names (c1, c2, h1, h2, s1, s2, etc.) in _compute_standalone_quarters() reduce readability. The pattern `h1/h2/h3/ha` for 'has Q1/Q2/Q3/annual' is not self-documenting.
fix_example:
col_q1, col_q2, col_q3, col_q4, col_ann = f'1Q{yy}', f'2Q{yy}', f'3Q{yy}', f'4Q{yy}', str(year)
has_q1 = col_q1 in df.columns
has_q2 = col_q2 in df.columns
# ...

description: print() in production code (also in src/utils.py validate_line_ids). Inconsistent with src/database.py and dashboard/loaders.py which use the logging module. Makes output suppression difficult in tests and production.
recommendation: Replace with `logger.info()` throughout src/standardizer.py and src/utils.py.
fix_example:
import logging
logger = logging.getLogger(__name__)
# ...
logger.info('[Standardizer] Dicionario carregado: %s contas canonicas', len(self._df))
